figures.title: AlphaZeroBeta: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Market-Neutral Portfolios
figures.caption: Fig. 1 AlphaZeroBeta architecture: a three-stage pipeline. Data Loading aggregates twelve het- erogeneous market and alternative data sources. Feature Engineering derives four alpha-signal families (momentum, mean reversion, volatility, and skew/jump) over rolling windows and normalizes them per sector and index into the input tensor. Backtesting formalizes the MDP loop: a Recur- rent PPO trading  — This figure illustrates the AlphaZeroBeta system architecture as a three-stage pipeline flowchart

figures.title: AlphaZeroBeta: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Market-Neutral Portfolios
figures.caption: Fig. 2 AlphaZeroBeta neural architecture. Three multi-resolution observation tensors (daily, weekly, and monthly), each resampled at the same close, are stacked along the channel axis and processed by a sequential CNN (8/4/3 kernels, 4/2/1 strides, 32/64/64 filters). figures.title: Optimal Market Making in Prediction Markets
figures.caption: Figure 2 shows the skew as a function of price for different inventory levels. At zero inventory, the skew is positive for prices below p = 1/2 and nega- tive for prices above it. Inventory shifts the skew in the expected direction as short positions move quotes upward, whereas long positions move them downward. Near the boundaries, the curves converge because risk vanishes as p(1 −p) →0. — This figure is a line graph showing skew (vertical axis) as a function of price p (horizontal axis, rangin

figures.title: Uniform-Loss Automated Market Making for Prediction Markets
figures.caption: Figure 1 compares these canonical AMMs with the PM-AMMs constructed from the Gaussian- score and most-exciting-game profiles. — This section presents mathematical formulations for two automated market maker (AMM) examples with uniform loss-value ratios. For the Constant Product Market Maker (CPMM), the pool-value function is V(p) = 2L√p(1-p), and the resulting price dynamics follow dP_t = P_t(1-P_t)/h(t) dW_t. figures.title: Determining Insolvency Regions in Banks: A Stochastic Dynamic Approach Integrating Liquidity and Credit Risk
figures.caption: Figure 1: Logical structure and computational workflow of the proposed model. — This flowchart illustrates the computational workflow for determining bank insolvency boundaries using stochastic optimization. The process begins with input data from CODAL databases and market data, flows through an optimization engine (SDP/RL) that maximizes an objective function subject to liquidity and solvency constraints, and produces an optimal value function. figures.title: Herding and Liquidity in Order-Book Markets. II. Fundamental Anchoring and the Resilience of Liquidity
figures.caption: Figure 1: The withdrawal mechanism is real. As the shared-risk weight λ rises, the market maker pulls quoted depth (left) and widens its half-spread (right) in the calm receiver B, and does so more strongly when the source market A is genuinely stressed than when A is calm.
